DIAGNOSIS EXPLANATION:
# Understanding Your Heart Condition

## Your Main Diagnosis

Your heart is not pumping blood as well as it should. This is called heart failure. Right now, your heart is weaker than normal.

Your heart has four chambers. The main pumping chamber is called the left ventricle. It is not squeezing as hard as it needs to. This means less blood gets pumped to your body.

When your heart cannot pump well, fluid backs up. This fluid can collect in your lungs and legs. That is why you may have felt short of breath or had swelling.

## Other Health Conditions You Have

**High Blood Pressure**
Your blood pressure has been higher than normal. This makes your heart work harder. Over time, this weakens your heart.

**Diabetes**
Your body has trouble controlling blood sugar. High blood sugar can damage your heart and blood vessels. This makes your heart weaker.

**Kidney Disease**
Your kidneys do not filter blood as well as they should. Your heart and kidneys are connected. When your heart is weak, your kidneys struggle. Your kidneys may also affect how well your heart works.

**High Cholesterol**
You have too much fat in your blood. This can clog your blood vessels. Clogged vessels make your heart work harder.

## What This Means

All of these conditions work together. They all made your heart weaker. The good news is that treatment can help. You will need to take your medicines as prescribed and follow your doctor's care plan.

MEDICATION RATIONALE:
Furosemide:
This medicine helps your body get rid of extra fluid. Your heart was not pumping well, so fluid built up. You may urinate more often than usual. This is normal and means the medicine is working. Call your doctor if you feel very dizzy, have muscle cramps, or notice your heart racing.

Lisinopril:
This medicine helps your heart pump more easily. It lowers the pressure your heart has to work against. You may feel lightheaded when you stand up. This usually goes away as your body adjusts. Call your doctor if your face or lips swell or if you have trouble breathing.

Carvedilol:
This medicine helps your heart work more efficiently. It slows your heart rate so your heart doesn't have to work as hard. You may feel tired or have cold hands and feet. These feelings often improve over time. Call your doctor if you feel very dizzy, faint, or have severe shortness of breath.

Spironolactone:
This medicine helps your body keep potassium and get rid of extra fluid. It protects your heart from further damage. You may urinate a bit more than usual. This is normal. Call your doctor if you feel weak, have muscle cramps, or feel confused.

Metoprolol:
This medicine slows your heart rate and lowers blood pressure. It helps your heart pump more efficiently. You may feel tired at first. This usually improves after a few weeks. Call your doctor if you feel very dizzy, faint, or have severe shortness of breath.

Metformin:
This medicine helps control your blood sugar levels. It works by helping your body use insulin better. You may have an upset stomach or diarrhea. These side effects often improve over time. Call your doctor if you have severe stomach pain or vomiting that doesn't stop.

RECOVERY TRAJECTORY:
**Week 1:**
Your heart is not pumping as strongly as it should. You will feel very tired and short of breath. This is normal right now. Rest as much as you need during the day. Walk for 5 minutes two times each day inside your home. Do not lift anything heavier than a coffee cup.

Weigh yourself every morning at the same time. Write down your weight. Call your doctor if you gain more than 2 pounds in one day. This means fluid is building up in your body. When you sit down, put your feet up on a pillow. This helps reduce swelling in your legs and feet.

**Week 2:**
You may start to feel a little less tired. Walking should feel easier. Try to walk for 10 minutes two times each day. You can do light activities like folding clothes or washing dishes. Stop right away if you feel short of breath. Rest for 15 minutes before doing something else.

Still do not lift anything heavier than 10 pounds. Your goal this week is to add 5 more minutes to your walks. Keep weighing yourself every morning. Keep your feet up when you sit. Eat less salt. Salt makes your body hold onto water.

**Week 3-4:**
You should feel stronger now. You can slowly do more around the house. Keep walking every day. Add a little more time each day if you feel good. Do light chores. Stop and rest if you get tired or short of breath.

Do not do hard exercise or heavy lifting yet. Eat foods with less salt. Drink only the amount of water your doctor told you to drink. Keep your feet up when you sit. Go to all your follow-up doctor visits. These visits are very important for your heart.

**When to expect improvement:**
Your shortness of breath should get better over the next 4 weeks. Keep taking all your heart medicines as your doctor prescribed. Call your doctor right away if you get worse instead of better.

ESCALATION GUIDE:
CALL 911 IMMEDIATELY
These symptoms are life-threatening. Do not drive yourself.
- Shortness of breath while resting or lying flat: Your heart cannot pump enough blood to your lungs.
- Waking up at night gasping for air: Your lungs are filling with fluid. You need help now.
- Lips, fingernails, or face turning blue or grey: Your body is not getting enough oxygen right now.
- Fast or irregular heartbeat with chest pain: This can mean your heart is in trouble.
- Sudden fainting or loss of consciousness: Your heart may not be pumping blood to your brain.
- Sudden severe confusion or disorientation: Your brain is not getting enough oxygen or blood.

GO TO THE ER TODAY
Do not wait until tomorrow. Go within a few hours.
- Sudden weight gain of more than 3 lbs in one day: Fluid is building up in your body too fast.
- Sudden weight gain of 5 lbs or more in a week: Your heart is not pumping out fluid well.
- Increased swelling in legs, ankles, or abdomen despite medication: Fluid is backing up even with your water pills.
- Fast or irregular heartbeat without chest pain: Your heart rhythm is not normal. You need to be checked.
- Sudden dizziness that does not go away: Your blood pressure or heart rhythm may be off.

CALL YOUR DOCTOR
Call during office hours. Do not go to the ER for these.
- Questions about your water pill or heart medicines: Your doctor will help you understand when to take them.
- Mild swelling that is slowly improving: This can happen during recovery but your doctor should know.
- Mild shortness of breath only with hard exercise: This is normal but tell your doctor at your next visit.

complete

DIAGNOSIS EXPLANATION:
# Your Hospital Diagnosis Explained

## What happened to your heart

Your heart was not pumping blood around your body well. This made fluid build up in your lungs and legs. We call this heart failure.

Your heart muscle is still strong. But it was stiff and could not relax. Stiff hearts cannot fill with blood the right way.

## Why this happened

You have three conditions that made your heart work harder:

**High blood pressure.** This forces your heart to squeeze harder every day. Over time, this makes the heart muscle stiff.

**Diabetes.** High blood sugar can damage blood vessels. This makes it harder for your heart to do its job.

**Sleep apnea.** When you stop breathing at night, your blood pressure goes up. This also stresses your heart.

## What you should know

Your heart needs help to work better. You may feel tired or short of breath. You may see swelling in your legs or feet.

These problems can get better with care and time. It is very important to follow up with your doctors. Take all your medicines as told.

## Next steps

Call your doctor if you gain weight quickly. Call if you cannot catch your breath. Call if swelling gets worse.

Your doctors will help you manage all of these conditions together.

MEDICATION RATIONALE:
Furosemide:
This medicine helps your body get rid of extra fluid. Your heart could not pump well, so fluid built up. You will urinate more often than usual. This is normal and means the medicine is working. Call your doctor if you feel very dizzy, have muscle cramps, or notice your heart racing.

Losartan:
This medicine helps lower your blood pressure. It takes pressure off your heart so it can work better. You may feel a little dizzy when you first stand up. This usually goes away after a few days. Call your doctor if you feel faint, have severe headaches, or have trouble breathing.

Metoprolol succinate:
This medicine slows your heart rate and lowers blood pressure. Your heart does not have to work as hard. You may feel tired or have less energy at first. This often improves after a few weeks. Call your doctor if you feel very short of breath, have a very slow heartbeat, or feel faint.

Spironolactone:
This medicine helps your body keep potassium and get rid of extra fluid. It protects your heart from damage. You may urinate a bit more often. You may also notice your potassium levels are higher. Call your doctor if you have muscle weakness, severe nausea, or an irregular heartbeat.

Atorvastatin:
This medicine lowers cholesterol in your blood. High cholesterol can damage your heart. You may not feel any change, but the medicine is still working. Most people tolerate this medicine very well. Call your doctor if you have muscle pain, weakness, or dark urine.

Dapagliflozin:
This medicine helps your heart pump better and reduces fluid buildup. It also helps control blood sugar if you have diabetes. You may urinate more often, especially at first. You may feel thirsty more than usual. Call your doctor if you have burning when you urinate, severe genital itching, or signs of infection.

RECOVERY TRAJECTORY:
**Week 1:**
Your heart is having trouble pumping fluid out of your body. You will feel very tired and may have trouble breathing. This is normal right after treatment. Do not lift anything heavier than a gallon of milk. Wear your compression stockings during the day to help with swelling. Use your CPAP machine every night as your doctor told you.

**Week 2:**
You may feel a little stronger than last week. Walking short distances inside your home is good. Stop walking if you feel very short of breath or dizzy. You can lift heavier items now, but do not overdo it. Keep wearing your compression stockings during the day. Keep using your CPAP machine every night without fail.

**Week 3-4:**
You can walk a little farther each day. Avoid hard exercise and high-intensity workouts for now. Go to all your follow-up appointments and blood pressure checks. Salt makes your heart work harder, so eat less salt. Use your compression stockings during the day. Never skip your CPAP machine at night.

**Week 5-6:**
You should feel noticeably better by now. Walking and light activities should feel easier. If shortness of breath or swelling gets worse, call your doctor right away. Keep taking all your medicines as prescribed. Check your weight each morning. Call your doctor if you gain more than 2 pounds in one day.

**When to expect improvement:**
You will start to feel better in 2 to 4 weeks. Healing takes time, so be patient with yourself. Follow all your doctor's orders and keep all appointments.

ESCALATION GUIDE:
CALL 911 IMMEDIATELY
These symptoms are life-threatening. Do not drive yourself.
- Inability to catch your breath while speaking: Your heart cannot pump blood well enough. You need oxygen right now.
- Chest pressure, tightness, or pain: This means your heart is in trouble. Do not wait.
- Confusion, cold/clammy skin, or blue-tinted lips: Your body is not getting enough oxygen. Call right now.
- New or worsening cough with pink, frothy sputum: Fluid is filling your lungs. This is a medical emergency.
- Severe dizziness or passing out: Your heart may not be pumping blood to your brain. Call right now.

GO TO THE ER TODAY
Do not wait until tomorrow. Go within a few hours.
- Significant increase in shortness of breath when lying flat: Your heart is getting worse. Go to the ER today.
- Rapid weight gain of more than 2 pounds overnight: Fluid is building up in your body. Your heart needs help.

CALL YOUR DOCTOR
Call during office hours. Do not go to the ER for these.
- No symptoms in this tier for your diagnosis.

You came to the hospital because your lungs were not working well. This is called an exacerbation. That means your lung condition got worse quickly.

You have a long-term lung disease. It makes it hard to breathe. Your airways get tight and fill with mucus. This happened because you smoked for many years.

## Why This Matters

Your lungs have trouble moving air in and out. This makes you feel short of breath. You may cough a lot. You may feel tired.

Right now, your lungs need help to work better. The hospital gave you medicines to open up your airways. You felt scared or worried about not being able to breathe. That is normal and understandable.

You also have high blood pressure. High blood pressure makes your heart work harder. It can make breathing problems worse too.

## Moving Forward

Your lungs can improve with the right care at home. Follow your discharge plan closely. Take your medicines as directed. Rest when you feel tired.

If you have more trouble breathing, call your doctor right away. Do not wait.

You quit smoking. That was a very smart choice. It will help your lungs heal.

MEDICATION RATIONALE:
Prednisone:
This medicine reduces swelling in your airways. Swelling made it hard for you to breathe. You will likely feel better within a few days. You may feel more hungry or have trouble sleeping. This is normal. Take this medicine exactly as your doctor said. After 5 days, you will taper it down slowly. Do not stop it suddenly without calling your doctor first.

Azithromycin:
This antibiotic fights the infection causing your breathing problems. You must take all 5 days of this medicine. Even if you feel better, finish the full course. You may have an upset stomach or diarrhea. This is common and not dangerous. Call your doctor if you have severe belly pain or bloody stools.

Tiotropium:
This medicine helps keep your airways open all day. You inhale it once daily using a special device. It prevents breathing problems before they start. You may cough a little after using it. This is normal. Call your doctor if you have sudden chest pain or trouble breathing.

Albuterol:
This medicine opens your airways quickly when you cannot breathe. Use it when you feel short of breath. You may feel your heart beat faster. This is normal and goes away quickly. Call your doctor if your heart races for hours or you feel shaky all day.

Fluticasone/Salmeterol:
This medicine prevents swelling and opens your airways. You must use it twice daily, even if you feel fine. It works best when used every day regularly. You may get a sore throat or hoarse voice. This is normal. Call your doctor if you have thrush or white patches in your mouth.

Amlodipine:
This medicine helps control your high blood pressure. Lowering blood pressure protects your heart and kidneys. You may feel slight headaches or have swollen feet. These are common and usually improve quickly. Call your doctor if you have chest pain or severe swelling.

RECOVERY TRAJECTORY:
**Week 1:**
You will feel tired and short of breath right now. This is normal after your lung flare-up. Rest at home for the first 3 to 5 days. Start walking slowly. Walk for 5 minutes twice a day inside your home. Avoid smoke, dust, strong fumes, and cold air. These can make your breathing worse.

**Week 2:**
You may feel a little stronger this week. Shortness of breath should improve slowly. Keep walking a bit longer each day. Do not lift heavy things or do hard exercise. If you feel dizzy or jittery, do not drive yet. Practice slow breathing when you walk or climb stairs.

**Week 3-4:**
You can do more light activities around your home. Keep walking every day. Do not do strenuous activity or heavy lifting yet. Avoid people who are sick. Go to all your follow-up appointments. Call your doctor if you cough up blood or feel worse.

**When to expect improvement:**
Most people feel better in 4 to 6 weeks. Keep taking your medicines as prescribed. Your breathing will get better slowly but steadily.

CALL 911 IMMEDIATELY
These symptoms are life-threatening. Do not drive yourself.

- Breathing does not improve or gets worse after using your rescue inhaler: Your airways are closing. You need help now.
- Confusion, difficulty speaking, or extreme drowsiness: Your brain is not getting enough oxygen.

- Chest pain or rapid, racing heartbeat: Your heart may not be getting enough oxygen.

GO TO THE ER TODAY

DIAGNOSIS EXPLANATION:
# Your Diagnosis Explained

## What Happened

You came to the hospital because your lungs got worse. This is called an exacerbation. Your lungs have a long-term condition called COPD. That means your lungs are damaged and it is hard to breathe.

## About Your Lungs

Your lungs help your body get air. With COPD, the tubes in your lungs get narrow. Air gets trapped inside. This makes it hard to breathe out.

You also have emphysema. This means parts of your lungs are damaged. The air sacs in your lungs are weak. This makes breathing even harder.

## Other Conditions You Have

You have acid reflux. This is when stomach acid comes up into your throat. It can feel like heartburn.

You also have a sleep condition. When you sleep, your airways sometimes close. This stops your breathing for short times. Then you wake up gasping.

## Moving Forward

Take care of your lungs. Avoid smoke and air pollution. Rest when you feel tired. Drink plenty of water.

If you have questions about your medicines, ask your doctor. Do not stop or change any medicine on your own.

MEDICATION RATIONALE:
Prednisone:
This medicine reduces swelling in your airways. The swelling made it hard for you to breathe. You may feel more energetic or sleep less. You may also feel hungrier than usual. These side effects go away when you stop the medicine. Take this for 7 days, then your doctor will have you take less each day. Call your doctor if you feel very anxious, confused, or have severe mood changes.

Doxycycline:
This antibiotic fights the infection making your breathing worse. It kills bacteria in your lungs. Take it for the full 7 days even if you feel better. You may have an upset stomach or feel nauseous. Take it with food or milk if your stomach hurts. Call your doctor if you have trouble swallowing, severe belly pain, or a rash.

Ipratropium/Albuterol (Combivent Respimat):
This inhaler medicine opens your airways so you breathe easier. It has two medicines that work together. Use it four times a day as scheduled. You may feel your heart beat a bit faster. Your mouth may feel dry. Call your doctor if you have chest pain, severe dizziness, or trouble urinating.

Budesonide/Formoterol (Symbicort):
This inhaler has a steroid that reduces airway swelling. It also has a medicine that opens your airways. Use it twice daily to keep your breathing steady. Your mouth may feel dry or you may get a hoarse voice. Rinse your mouth with water after each use. Call your doctor if you have trouble breathing, fever, or signs of infection.

Omeprazole:
This medicine protects your stomach from acid damage. It lowers the amount of acid your stomach makes. Take it once a day as directed. You may feel less heartburn or stomach pain. You may feel bloated or have gas. Call your doctor if you have severe belly pain, vomit blood, or have black stools.

Guaifenesin:
This medicine helps loosen thick mucus in your lungs. Thinner mucus is easier to cough up. Take it every 12 hours when you need it. Drink plenty of water to help it work better. You should not have any bothersome side effects. Call your doctor if you cough up blood or have severe chest pain.

RECOVERY TRAJECTORY:
**Week 1:**
You will feel tired and short of breath. This is normal after being in the hospital. Rest as much as you need. Walk inside your home for 5 to 10 minutes three times each day. Use your oxygen at night and when you move around. Call your doctor if you cough up blood or cannot catch your breath at rest.

**Week 2:**
You should have a little more energy now. Your breathing may still feel hard with activity. Keep walking inside your home. Do not go to crowded places yet. Stay away from people who are sick. Your goal this week is to walk a little farther each day.

**Week 3-4:**
You can do light activities around your house. You may feel ready to go outside. Do not push yourself too hard. Keep using your oxygen at night. Avoid heavy lifting and strenuous exercise. Go to all your follow-up appointments with your doctor.

**Week 5-6:**
You should start to feel stronger. Shortness of breath with light activity should be getting better. Keep taking all your inhalers exactly as shown to you. Avoid sick people and crowded places. Do not smoke or be around smoke. Call your doctor if you start feeling worse instead of better.

**When to expect improvement:**
You will slowly feel better over several weeks. Keep your head elevated when resting to help you breathe easier. Use your oxygen, take your medicines, and do your breathing exercises every day.

ESCALATION GUIDE:
CALL 911 IMMEDIATELY
These symptoms are life-threatened. Do not drive yourself.
- Sudden, sharp chest pain: This can mean a serious lung or heart problem.
- Extreme difficulty breathing or cannot catch your breath: Your body is not getting enough oxygen right now.
- Use of neck or chest muscles to help breathe: Your lungs are working too hard. You need help now.
- Coughing up bloody mucus: This can mean bleeding in your lungs.
- Dizziness, lightheadedness, or feeling like you might pass out: Your brain is not getting enough oxygen.

GO TO THE ER TODAY
Do not wait until tomorrow. Go within a few hours.
- Coughing up yellow or green mucus that is getting worse: This can mean a bad lung infection.
- Inability to sleep due to shortness of breath even with oxygen: Your breathing is not getting better with treatment.
- Significant increase in rescue inhaler use without relief: Your rescue inhaler is not working like it should.
- Increased swelling in your ankles, feet, or legs: This can mean your heart is having trouble pumping.

CALL YOUR DOCTOR
Call during office hours. Do not go to the ER for these.

DIAGNOSIS EXPLANATION:
# Understanding Your Diagnosis

Your body has trouble controlling blood sugar. This condition is called Type 1 Diabetes.

## What Happened

Your pancreas is an organ that makes insulin. Insulin helps your body use sugar for energy. In Type 1 Diabetes, your pancreas cannot make enough insulin. So sugar builds up in your blood instead of being used.

## Why You Felt Sick

High blood sugar caused several things to happen:

**You urinated a lot.** Your kidneys tried to get rid of extra sugar. This made you pee more than normal.

**You felt very thirsty.** Losing so much fluid made your body need more water.

**You lost weight.** Your body could not use the sugar for energy. So it burned other things instead.

**You were dehydrated.** All that urination caused you to lose water faster than you drank it.

## Going Forward

Type 1 Diabetes is a serious condition. But it can be managed well. Your doctors will help you learn how to take care of yourself. Ask your doctor or nurse about next steps. They will explain your treatment plan and answer your questions.

MEDICATION RATIONALE:
Insulin glargine:
This medicine helps control your blood sugar all day and night. You inject it under your skin once every night before bed. Your blood sugar should stay more stable and even. You may feel less tired or thirsty over time. Call your doctor if you feel shaky, sweaty, or confused, as these may be signs of low blood sugar.

Insulin lispro:
This medicine helps control your blood sugar right after you eat. You inject it under your skin before each meal three times daily. Your blood sugar should come down after meals. You may feel less hungry or thirsty. Call your doctor if you feel shaky, sweaty, or confused, as these may be signs of low blood sugar.

Glucagon Emergency Kit:
This kit is for your family to use if you pass out from very low blood sugar. Your family member will inject the glucagon to raise your blood sugar fast. You should wake up and feel better within ten to fifteen minutes. Keep this kit at home and teach your family how to use it. Call 911 or go to the ER immediately if you experience severe low blood sugar symptoms like loss of consciousness or seizures.

RECOVERY TRAJECTORY:
**Week 1:**
Your blood sugar has been high. The new insulin plan will help bring it down slowly. You will feel tired as your body adjusts to the new medicine. Rest as much as you need. Start walking a little bit each day inside your home. Check your blood sugar before and after you walk. Write down all your numbers in your logbook.

**Week 2:**
You may have more energy this week. Your blood sugar numbers should start to get better. Do not exercise hard yet. Walk a bit longer than last week if you feel okay. Always carry glucose tablets or juice with you. Check your blood sugar before you exercise. If your number is above 250, do not exercise that day.

**Week 3-4:**
You can do light activities around your home now. Go back to your normal routine slowly. Keep checking your blood sugar before and after exercise. Write down everything in your logbook. Make sure your family knows how to help if you need it. Bring your logbook to all your doctor visits.

**When to expect improvement:**
Your blood sugar will get better over the next few weeks. Keep taking your insulin exactly as your doctor told you. Call your doctor right away if you feel very sick or very tired.

ESCALATION GUIDE:
CALL 911 IMMEDIATELY
These symptoms are life-threatening. Do not drive yourself.
- Confusion, extreme sleepiness, or loss of consciousness: Your brain needs sugar and oxygen to work right now.
- Rapid, deep breathing or fruity smelling breath: This means your blood is too acidic and you need help fast.
- Severe low blood sugar below 55 mg/dL that does not improve after eating 15 grams of carbs: Your brain will not work without sugar. Use your glucagon kit if you cannot eat.
- Presence of moderate to large ketones in urine: This means your body is burning fat too fast and making poison in your blood.

GO TO THE ER TODAY
Do not wait until tomorrow. Go within a few hours.
- Blood sugar level above 400 mg/dL that does not respond to correction insulin: High blood sugar for too long can damage your body.
- Persistent vomiting or inability to keep fluids down: You need fluids and medicine to stay safe and stable.

CALL YOUR DOCTOR
Call during office hours. Do not go to the ER for these.

# Your Diagnosis Explained

## Your Main Condition: Type 2 Diabetes

Your body has trouble controlling blood sugar. Blood sugar is the fuel your body uses for energy. When you have diabetes, your body either cannot make enough insulin or cannot use it well. Insulin is a helper that moves sugar from your blood into your cells.

Right now, your blood sugar is higher than it should be. This happened during your hospital stay. Your doctors want to help bring it back down.

## Your Other Conditions

**High Blood Pressure**
The force of blood pushing on your blood vessel walls is too strong. This can harm your heart and kidneys over time.

**High Cholesterol**
You have too much fat in your blood. This fat can build up in your blood vessels.

**Dehydration**
Your body did not have enough water. You may feel tired or dizzy. Drinking more water can help.

## What Comes Next

Talk to your doctor about all your medicines. Take them as prescribed. Eat healthy foods and drink water. Move your body each day if you can. Come back for follow-up visits with your doctor.

Your doctor will help you manage these conditions. You are not alone in this.

MEDICATION RATIONALE:
Metformin:
This medicine helps your body use insulin better. Insulin is a hormone that controls your blood sugar. Your blood sugar has been too high, so this medicine helps bring it down. You might have an upset stomach or diarrhea at first. These side effects usually go away after a few days. Call your doctor if you have severe stomach pain, vomiting, or trouble breathing.

Glimepiride:
This medicine makes your pancreas release more insulin. Your pancreas is the organ that controls blood sugar. It helps lower your blood sugar throughout the day. You might feel shaky, sweaty, or confused if your blood sugar gets too low. This is called low blood sugar. Eat or drink something with sugar right away if this happens. Call your doctor if low blood sugar happens often or if you feel chest pain.

Insulin glargine:
This medicine provides insulin your body needs to control blood sugar. You inject it under your skin once every night before bed. It works slowly over 24 hours to keep your blood sugar steady. You might feel a small sting when you inject it. Slight redness or bruising at the injection site is normal. Call your doctor if you have signs of low blood sugar like shakiness, sweating, or confusion. Call 911 right away if you feel chest pain or have trouble breathing.

Lisinopril:
This medicine helps lower your blood pressure. High blood pressure can damage your heart and kidneys over time. You might feel a bit tired or dizzy at first. A dry cough is common and usually goes away. These side effects are not dangerous. Call your doctor if your face or lips swell, or if you feel very dizzy or faint.

Atorvastatin:
This medicine lowers cholesterol in your blood. High cholesterol can clog your arteries and cause heart problems. You may not feel any change, but the medicine is protecting your heart. Some people get muscle aches or feel tired. These side effects are usually mild. Call your doctor if you have severe muscle pain or weakness, or if your urine is dark colored.

RECOVERY TRAJECTORY:
**Week 1:**
You just learned you have high blood sugar. Your body needs time to adjust to new medicines. You will feel tired. This is normal. Walk for 10 to 15 minutes each day inside your home. Check your feet every day for red spots or blisters.

**Week 2:**
You may have more energy this week. Keep checking your blood sugar as taught in the hospital. Walk a little longer each day. Try to walk 20 to 30 minutes daily. Wear shoes that fit well at all times. Do not walk barefoot indoors or outdoors.

**Week 3-4:**
You are learning how your body responds to food. Keep up your daily walks. Check your feet every single day using a mirror if needed. Always wear proper shoes. Before you drive or use any machinery, check your blood sugar first. Tell your doctor if your blood sugar stays very high.

**When to expect improvement:**
Your blood sugar will get better over weeks and months. Keep taking all your medicines as prescribed. Call your doctor if you feel dizzy or very tired.

ESCALATION GUIDE:
CALL 911 IMMEDIATELY
These symptoms are life-threatening. Do not drive yourself.
- Blood sugar above 400 mg/dL or "HI" on meter: This can mean diabetic emergency. Your blood is too acidic.
- Severe low blood sugar with confusion and sweating: Your brain needs sugar right now. This is dangerous.
- Shortness of breath or chest pain: Your heart may not be working right. Minutes matter.
- Sudden blurred vision: This can mean a stroke or eye emergency. Get help now.

GO TO THE ER TODAY
Do not wait until tomorrow. Go within a few hours.
- Non-healing wounds or sores on feet or legs: These can get infected fast with diabetes. Check them now.
- Significant redness on feet or legs that is spreading: This means your skin is in trouble. Do not wait.
- Numbness or tingling in hands or feet: This can mean nerve damage is getting worse. Your doctor needs to see you.

CALL YOUR DOCTOR
Call during office hours. Do not go to the ER for these.
- Extreme thirst and frequent urination despite taking medicine: Your blood sugar may still be too high. Tell your doctor.

DIAGNOSIS EXPLANATION:
# Your Primary Diagnosis Explained

## What Happened

You had severe wear and tear in your right hip joint. The smooth surfaces inside your hip were damaged. This made movement painful and stiff.

Your doctor fixed this by replacing your hip joint with an artificial one. This surgery is called a hip replacement.

## Why This Happened

Hip wear and tear can happen over time. Extra weight on your joints can make it worse. You also have other health conditions that may play a role.

## What to Know Going Forward

Your new hip joint should work much better. You should have less pain and stiffness.

You also have other health conditions we want you to know about:
- **Diabetes**: Your blood sugar is well-controlled right now. Keep taking your medicines as prescribed.
- **High blood pressure**: This is being managed. Keep taking your medicines as prescribed.
- **Low blood count**: You had fewer red blood cells before surgery. This is common and often improves on its own.

## Next Steps

Follow your doctor's instructions for hip recovery. Ask your doctor or therapist about safe exercises. Attend all follow-up visits.

If you have questions about your medicines or any new symptoms, call your doctor right away.

MEDICATION RATIONALE:
Enoxaparin:
This medicine thins your blood to stop clots from forming. Your hip surgery or injury puts you at risk for blood clots. You will give yourself shots under your skin once a day. Bruising and mild soreness at the injection site is normal. Call your doctor right away if you have severe bleeding, large bruises, or blood in your urine or stool.

Celecoxib:
This medicine reduces pain and swelling in your hip joint. It helps you move more easily so you can do physical therapy. You may have mild stomach upset or heartburn. Taking it with food can help. Call your doctor if you have severe stomach pain, dark or bloody stools, or chest pain.

Oxycodone:
This strong medicine reduces severe pain from your hip arthritis. Take it every 6 hours only when you need it for pain. You may feel drowsy, dizzy, or constipated. These are normal side effects. Do not drive or use machines after taking this medicine. Call your doctor if you feel confused, very drowsy, or have trouble breathing.

Metformin:
This medicine helps control your blood sugar levels. Take one tablet by mouth twice a day. You may have mild stomach upset or diarrhea at first. This usually goes away. Call your doctor if the stomach problems get worse or do not stop.

Lisinopril:
This medicine helps your heart work better and lowers blood pressure. Take one tablet by mouth once a day. You may have a dry cough. This is common and is not dangerous. Call your doctor if your face or lips swell, or if you feel dizzy.

Pantoprazole:
This medicine protects your stomach from acid damage. Take one tablet by mouth once a day. You may have mild headache or diarrhea. These are normal side effects. Call your doctor if you have severe stomach pain or blood in your stool.

RECOVERY TRAJECTORY:
**Week 1:**
You will feel tired and sore after your hip surgery. This is normal and will get better. Do not bend your hip more than a right angle (like when you sit in a chair). Do not cross your legs or twist your body. Use your walker for all walking. Your goal this week is to do your ankle pumps every hour while awake. Squeeze your thigh muscles and buttock muscles three times a day as your therapist showed you.

**Week 2:**
You may have less pain this week. Walking with your walker should feel easier. Keep your hip precautions the same. Do not bend your hip past a right angle. Do not cross your legs. Do your ankle pumps, thigh squeezes, and buttock squeezes every day. Your goal this week is to walk a little farther with your walker each day.

**Week 3-4:**
You should feel stronger now. Your pain should be getting better. Keep using your walker for now. Keep your hip precautions. Do your exercises as your physical therapist showed you. Do not rush to stop using your walker yet. Your goal this week is to keep walking and doing your exercises every day.

**Week 5-6:**
You may be ready to use a cane instead of a walker. Ask your physical therapist when you can switch. Your hip should feel more stable. Keep your hip precautions until your doctor says to stop. Do not cross your legs yet. Walk more each day with your cane. Your goal is to walk without pain or limping.

**When to expect improvement:**
You will feel better a little more each week. Most healing takes 6 to 12 weeks. Call your doctor if you have more swelling, more pain, or trouble walking.

ESCALATION GUIDE:
CALL 911 IMMEDIATELY
These symptoms are life-threatening. Do not drive yourself.
- Sudden shortness of breath or chest pain: These can mean a blood clot has reached your lungs.
- Rapid heart rate with chest pain: Your heart may not be getting enough blood right now.
- Sudden severe hip pain and cannot bear weight: Your new hip may have slipped out of place.
- Visible deformity in your hip or leg: This means your hip joint is not in the right spot.
- Extreme drowsiness or slow or stopped breathing: Your pain medicine is too strong and your body is shutting down.

GO TO THE ER TODAY
Do not wait until tomorrow. Go within a few hours.
- Calf pain with swelling or redness: This can mean a blood clot is forming in your leg.
- Fever above 101.5 degrees F that will not go away: Your body may have an infection from surgery.
- Increased pain, redness, or warmth at incision: Your surgical cut may be getting infected.
- Foul-smelling drainage from your incision: This is a sign your incision is infected.
- Numbness, tingling, or weakness in your operated leg: A nerve near your surgery may be damaged or swollen.

CALL YOUR DOCTOR
Call during office hours. Do not go to the ER for these.
- Questions about your pain medicine or blood thinner: Your doctor will help you use them safely and well.

DIAGNOSIS EXPLANATION:
# Your Diagnosis Explained

## Your Hip Problem

Your left hip bone is not getting enough blood. This is called avascular necrosis. Without enough blood, the bone starts to break down. This causes pain and makes walking hard.

Your doctors fixed this during your hospital stay. They replaced your hip with an artificial one. This should help you move better and hurt less.

## Other Health Things to Know

**Your Kidneys**
Your kidneys are not working as well as normal. They still do their job, but not at full strength. You should drink plenty of water. Talk to your doctor before taking any new pills.

**Your Cholesterol**
Your body makes too much cholesterol. This is a fat in your blood. High cholesterol can hurt your heart. Your doctor may have given you a pill to help.

**Your Stomach**
You have acid reflux. This means acid from your stomach comes up your throat. It can feel like burning in your chest. Eat smaller meals. Avoid spicy foods and foods that bother you.

**Your Mood**
You have mild depression. This is being treated with medicine. It is stable right now. Keep taking your pills as ordered.

MEDICATION RATIONALE:
Aspirin:
This medicine helps prevent blood clots after your hip surgery. Blood clots can form when you are not moving much. You may notice your stomach feels slightly upset sometimes. This is normal with aspirin. Call your doctor if you have severe stomach pain, vomiting blood, or black stools.

Gabapentin:
This medicine helps reduce nerve pain from your hip condition. It works by calming down pain signals in your body. You may feel sleepy or dizzy at first. This usually gets better as your body adjusts. Call your doctor if you feel very confused, have trouble walking, or feel very dizzy.

Tramadol:
This medicine helps ease your pain when it gets bad. You only take it when you need it, up to every 8 hours. You may feel drowsy or dizzy after taking it. This is normal and means the medicine is working. Do not drive or use machines right after taking it. Call your doctor if you feel very confused, very sleepy, or have trouble breathing.

Atorvastatin:
This medicine helps keep your cholesterol at a healthy level. High cholesterol can damage your heart and blood vessels. You probably will not feel any change in your body. This is normal—the medicine works quietly over time. Call your doctor if you have muscle pain that is severe or does not go away.

Omeprazole:
This medicine reduces acid in your stomach to prevent ulcers. Your stomach will feel less irritated and uncomfortable. You may notice your digestion feels better. This is normal and means the medicine is working. Call your doctor if you have severe stomach pain or bloody stools.

Sertraline:
This medicine helps improve your mood and reduce anxiety. It can take two to four weeks to feel the full effect. You may feel slightly nauseous or have headaches at first. These side effects usually fade within a few days. Call your doctor if you have thoughts of hurting yourself or feel very confused.

RECOVERY TRAJECTORY:
**Week 1:**
You will feel very tired and sore after your surgery. Your hip will hurt when you move it. This is normal. Take your pain medicine as your doctor prescribed. Do your ankle pumps every hour while you are awake. This keeps blood clots from forming. Squeeze your buttock muscles and tighten your thigh muscles three times, 15 squeezes each, twice a day.

Walk a little bit with your walker or crutches as your physical therapist showed you. You can put your full weight on your new hip. Do not take big steps or walk on stairs yet. Do not bend your hip more than 90 degrees. Call your doctor if you have more swelling or feel feverish.

**Week 2:**
You should start to feel a little less pain. You may still feel very tired. Keep doing your ankle pumps and leg exercises every day. Walk more each day, even if it is slow. Stand up and do the hip moves your therapist taught you. Keep your leg moving to prevent blood clots.

You can walk on flat ground with your walker. Do not walk on stairs or uneven ground yet. Do not twist or turn your hips sharply. Do not lean over or cross your legs. Your goal this week is to walk a little farther each day. Call your doctor if you feel heat or redness around your hip.

**Week 3-4:**
You will feel stronger and have less pain now. Most of your soreness should be getting better. Keep doing all your exercises at home every day. You may be able to use a cane instead of a walker. Walk on flat surfaces as much as you can.

Do not run, jump, or play sports. Do not do high-impact activities. Stand and do hip moves as your therapist showed you. You can sit in a regular chair now. Your goal this week is to walk without your walker if your therapist says it is okay. Call your doctor before you try anything new.

**Week 5-6:**
You will have much less pain now. Walking should feel more normal. Keep doing your home exercises every day. You can talk to your surgeon about starting to use a stationary bike. Do not start biking unless your surgeon says it is okay.

You can go back to light daily activities around your home. Do not do heavy work or lifting. Do not bend your hip more than 90 degrees. Do not cross your legs. Do not twist your hips. Ask your surgeon before you try new activities. Your goal is to go to all your follow-up appointments.

**When to expect improvement:**
You should feel much better by 6 weeks. Pain and swelling will slowly get better over 3 to 6 months. Keep taking your medicines as prescribed and do your exercises every day.

ESCALATION GUIDE:
CALL 911 IMMEDIATELY
These symptoms are life-threatening. Do not drive yourself.
- Chest pain with shortness of breath: This can mean a blood clot reached your lungs. It is very dangerous.
- Coughing up blood: Your lungs may be bleeding. This needs emergency help right now.
- Sudden trouble walking or severe hip deformity: Your new hip may have popped out of place. This is an emergency.
- Throat swelling or trouble breathing: This is a severe allergic reaction. It can block your airway.

GO TO THE ER TODAY
Do not wait until tomorrow. Go within a few hours.
- One-sided leg swelling, pain, or calf redness: This can mean a blood clot in your leg.
- Fever above 101 F lasting more than 24 hours: This can mean your body has a serious infection.
- Pus, warmth, or foul odor at your incision: Your wound may be infected. You need to be checked today.
- Hives or rash after taking a medicine: This is an allergic reaction. Stop the medicine and go to the ER.

CALL YOUR DOCTOR
Call during office hours. Do not go to the ER for these.
- Decreased urine output: Your kidneys may not be working well. Tell your doctor right away.
- Significant swelling in hands or feet: This can affect your kidney function. Your doctor needs to know.

DIAGNOSIS EXPLANATION:
# Your Hospital Diagnosis Explained

## Main Condition: Gallbladder Infection

You had an infection in your gallbladder. This happened because a small stone got stuck inside it. The gallbladder is a small bag under your liver. It helps your body digest fats.

When a stone blocks it, the gallbladder gets red and swollen. This causes pain and infection. Doctors found a stone also stuck in a tube that drains bile. Bile is a fluid that helps you digest food.

## Other Health Conditions You Have

**Type 2 Diabetes:** Your body has trouble controlling blood sugar. Blood sugar is the fuel that runs your body.

**High Blood Pressure:** The force of blood pushing on your vessels is too high. This can tire out your heart over time.

**Slow Digestion:** After your hospital stay, your belly is moving food slowly. This is normal after certain procedures. It should get better soon.

---

## What to Know

Your doctors treated the gallbladder problem during your stay. Your body is now healing. Keep taking all your medicines as told. Call your doctor if you have new pain or problems. You are on the path to feeling better.

MEDICATION RATIONALE:
Metformin:
This medicine helps control your blood sugar levels. You were taking it before, so keep taking it the same way. Take it twice daily with your meals. You may have a mild stomach upset at first. Call your doctor if you have severe stomach pain, vomiting, or trouble breathing.

Lisinopril:
This medicine helps lower your blood pressure. It takes pressure off your heart. You were taking it before, so keep taking it the same way. You may feel slightly dizzy or tired at first. Call your doctor if you feel very dizzy, faint, or if your face swells.

Oxycodone/Acetaminophen:
This medicine helps with severe pain after your surgery. It works best if you take it before pain gets very bad. You may feel drowsy or dizzy. Do not drive or use machinery while taking this medicine. Only take this for up to 5 days. Call your doctor if you have trouble breathing, severe drowsiness, or feel confused.

Ibuprofen:
This medicine reduces pain and swelling after your oxycodone course ends. Take it with food to protect your stomach. You may have a mild stomach upset. This is normal. Take it every 8 hours as needed. Call your doctor if you have black or bloody stools, severe stomach pain, or vomit that looks like coffee grounds.

Metronidazole:
This antibiotic (infection-fighting medicine) treats a bacterial infection in your bile ducts. Finish all 5 days of this medicine even if you feel better. You may notice a metallic taste in your mouth. Your urine may turn dark brown or reddish. This is normal. Call your doctor if you have severe nausea, vomiting, numbness in your hands or feet, or a rash.

Ondansetron:
This medicine helps stop nausea and vomiting. Take it every 8 hours as needed. Most people do not have side effects from this medicine. It works quickly to calm your stomach. Call your doctor if nausea does not improve after two doses.

Docusate Sodium:
This medicine softens your stool to prevent constipation. Pain medicine can make you constipated, so this helps. Take it twice daily. Your bowel movements should become easier and softer. Call your doctor if you have severe belly pain or cannot have a bowel movement after one week.

Omeprazole:
This medicine protects your stomach lining from acid damage. Take it once daily before breakfast. It helps prevent stomach upset from pain medicine and other medicines. You may have mild headache or stomach discomfort at first. Call your doctor if you have severe stomach pain, black stools, or vomit that looks like coffee grounds.

RECOVERY TRAJECTORY:
**Week 1:**
You will feel very tired and sore after surgery. This is normal. Rest as much as you need. Walk slowly around your home a few times each day. Do not lift anything heavier than a cup of water. Keep your belly wound clean and dry. You can shower after 48 hours. Pat the wound dry with a clean towel. Call your doctor if you see red streaks, swelling, or pus around your wound.

**Week 2:**
You should start to feel a little better. Your belly may still be tender. Walk a bit longer each day, but stay close to home. Do not lift more than 5 pounds for 4 weeks total. Do not drive yet, especially while taking pain medicine. Your goal this week is to walk a little farther than last week. Call your doctor if you have fever, chills, or belly pain that gets worse.

**Week 3-4:**
You can do light tasks around the house now. Keep walking every day to build your strength back. Do not lift heavy things or do hard exercise yet. You can drive again if you have stopped taking strong pain medicine. Avoid baths, pools, and hot tubs until your wound is fully healed. Your goal is to feel strong enough for short outings. Call your doctor if you feel dizzy, very tired, or have new belly pain.

**When to expect improvement:**
Your belly wound should feel much better by 4 weeks. You will have more energy each week. Keep your follow-up appointments so your doctor can check your healing.

ESCALATION GUIDE:
CALL 911 IMMEDIATELY
These symptoms are life-threatening. Do not drive yourself.
- Shortness of breath or chest pain: Your heart may not be getting enough oxygen right now.
- Severe abdominal pain that is getting worse: This can mean a serious problem inside your belly.
- Incision edges separating or wound opening: Your surgical cut is not healing. Infection can spread fast.
- Persistent vomiting lasting more than 24 hours: You cannot keep fluids in your body.
- Inability to tolerate any oral intake for more than 12 hours: Your body needs food and water to survive.
- Blood sugar below 70 mg/dL with symptoms: Low blood sugar can make you pass out or shake badly.

GO TO THE ER TODAY
Do not wait until tomorrow. Go within a few hours.
- Fever above 101 degrees F at any point: This means your body is fighting an infection.
- Increasing redness, warmth, swelling, or discharge from incision: Your wound is showing signs of infection.
- Jaundice — yellowing of skin or eyes: This means bile is not draining from your liver.
- Dark urine or pale clay-coloured stools: Your bile duct may still be blocked.
- Blood sugar above 300 mg/dL: This is too high even for a diabetic.

CALL YOUR DOCTOR
Call during office hours. Do not go to the ER for these.
- Mild nausea without vomiting: This can happen after surgery but your doctor should know.
- Questions about your pain medicine or other medicines: Your doctor will help you take them safely.
- Slow wound healing without signs of infection: This is normal but your doctor should check it.
- Mild constipation: Your bowel may still be slow after surgery and medicine.

Your body will make more red blood cells on its own.
This takes a few weeks.

You also felt sick to your stomach.
You threw up a few times.
This is very common after surgery.

DIAGNOSIS EXPLANATION:
# Understanding Your Diagnosis

## Your Main Condition: Heart Failure

Your heart is not pumping as well as it should right now. This is called heart failure. Your heart muscle is weaker than normal. It cannot move blood through your body as easily.

When the heart is weak, fluid builds up. This fluid can go into your lungs. It can also cause swelling in your legs and feet. You may feel tired or short of breath.

## Why This Happened

You have several conditions that stress your heart. High blood pressure makes your heart work harder. Over time, this weakens the heart muscle. Diabetes also harms the heart. High cholesterol does too.

Your kidneys are also weaker than they should be. Weak kidneys and a weak heart affect each other. Both need to work together.

## What Comes Next

Your body needs time to heal. Your doctors gave you medicines to help. These medicines will help your heart work better. They will also help remove extra fluid.

You will need check-ups to see how you are doing. Your doctor will watch your heart closely.

## Take Care of Yourself

Rest when you feel tired. Try to stay calm and reduce stress. Your doctor will talk to you about diet and activity. Follow those directions carefully.

If you feel worse, call your doctor right away. Do not wait.

MEDICATION RATIONALE:
Furosemide:
This medicine helps your body get rid of extra fluid. Your heart was not pumping well, so fluid built up in your body. You will urinate more often than usual. This is normal and means the medicine is working. Call your doctor if you feel very dizzy, have muscle cramps, or notice your heart racing.

Lisinopril:
This medicine helps your heart pump more easily. It lowers the pressure your heart has to work against. You may feel a dry cough or feel dizzy at first. These side effects usually go away over time. Call your doctor if your face or lips swell up, or if you have trouble breathing.

Carvedilol:
This medicine helps your heart work more efficiently and steadily. It slows your heart rate so your heart does not have to work as hard. You may feel tired or a little dizzy. These feelings are normal and usually improve. Call your doctor if you feel very short of breath, have severe dizziness, or your heart beats very slowly.

Spironolactone:
This medicine helps your body keep potassium and get rid of extra sodium. It helps reduce the swelling and strain on your heart. You may need to urinate a bit more. This is normal. Call your doctor if you feel weak, have muscle cramps, or notice irregular heartbeats.

Metoprolol:
This medicine helps your heart pump more efficiently and at a steady rate. It also helps lower your blood pressure. You may feel tired or a little dizzy at first. These side effects usually improve over time. Call your doctor if you feel very short of breath, have severe chest pain, or your heart feels very slow.

Metformin:
This medicine helps control your blood sugar levels. It helps your body use insulin more effectively. You may have a mild upset stomach or diarrhea at first. Taking it with food may help reduce this. Call your doctor if you have severe stomach pain, unusual muscle pain, or difficulty breathing.

RECOVERY TRAJECTORY:
**Week 1:**
Your heart needs time to recover from this hospitalization. You will feel very tired. This is normal. Start by walking 5 minutes twice a day inside your home. Rest often between activities. Do not lift anything heavier than a full cup of coffee.

Swelling in your legs and ankles is common right now. Put your legs up on pillows when you sit down. This helps fluid move back to your heart. Watch your weight every morning. Call your doctor if you gain more than 2 pounds in one day.

Shortness of breath when you move around is normal this week. But stop moving if you feel short of breath. Rest until you feel better. Your goal this week is to take your medicines on time every day.

**Week 2:**

**Week 3-4:**
You can walk a little farther now. Try walking 15 to 20 minutes once or twice a day. You can do more around the house. But avoid heavy lifting and hard exercise. Take rest breaks when you need them. Your goal this week is to go to all your doctor visits.

You should feel much less short of breath now. If shortness of breath gets worse instead of better, call your doctor. Keep taking all your heart medicines exactly as prescribed. Do not stop or change any medicine on your own. Weigh yourself every day and call your doctor with any big changes.

**When to expect improvement:**
You will start to feel better each week. After 4 to 6 weeks, you should have much more energy. Keep taking your medicines and follow your salt and fluid limits. Call your doctor if anything feels wrong.

CALL 911 IMMEDIATELY

DIAGNOSIS EXPLANATION:
# Understanding Your Hospital Visit

## What Happened

Your heart had trouble pumping blood around your body. This made fluid build up in your lungs and legs. That is why you felt short of breath.

Your heart muscle itself is still strong. The problem is that it got too stiff. A stiff heart cannot relax and fill with blood the way it should.

## Why This Happened

You have several conditions that made your heart work harder:

**High blood pressure.** This forces your heart to pump against more force. Over time, this makes the heart muscle thicker and stiffer.

**Diabetes.** High blood sugar can damage your heart over time. It also makes high blood pressure worse.

**Extra weight.** This puts strain on your heart and makes it work harder.

**Sleep apnea.** When you stop breathing at night, your heart gets stressed. This can make heart problems worse.

All of these together caused your heart to fail.

## What You Need to Know

Your heart needs rest and care right now. Follow your doctor's plan carefully. Take all your medicines as prescribed.

Watch for warning signs. Call your doctor if you feel more short of breath. Also call if your legs swell up or you gain weight fast.

Your doctor will help you feel better. You can recover with the right care.

MEDICATION RATIONALE:
Furosemide:
This medicine helps your body get rid of extra fluid. Your heart was not pumping well, so fluid built up. You may urinate more often. This is normal and means the medicine is working. Call your doctor if you feel very dizzy, have muscle cramps, or notice your heart beating very fast.

Losartan:
This medicine lowers the pressure around your heart. It helps your heart work more easily. You may feel a little dizzy at first. This usually goes away as your body gets used to it. Call your doctor if you have a dry cough, feel very dizzy, or have swelling in your face or lips.

Metoprolol succinate:
This medicine helps your heart beat slower and with less force. A slower heartbeat means your heart does not have to work so hard. You may feel tired or have less energy. These feelings often improve over time. Call your doctor if you feel dizzy, have trouble breathing, or your heartbeat feels very slow or uneven.

Spironolactone:
This medicine helps your body hold onto potassium. It also helps remove extra fluid and salt. You should not eat a lot of salt. Your doctor may want to check your blood potassium levels. Call your doctor if you feel dizzy, very weak, or have an irregular heartbeat.

Atorvastatin:
This medicine helps lower cholesterol in your blood. Lower cholesterol helps protect your heart and blood vessels. You may not notice anything right away. That is normal for this type of medicine. Call your doctor if you have muscle pain, weakness, or very dark urine.

Dapagliflozin:
This medicine helps your heart work better and removes extra fluid. It was added to help your heart pump more effectively. You may urinate more often. This is expected and is how the medicine helps you. Call your doctor if you feel dizzy, very thirsty, have trouble urinating, or have unusual tiredness.

RECOVERY TRAJECTORY:
**Week 1:**
Your heart is not pumping fluid out as well as it should. You will feel very tired and short of breath. This is normal right now. Do not lift anything heavier than a gallon of milk. Wear your compression stockings during the day. Use your CPAP machine every single night.

**Week 2:**
You may feel a little less tired than last week. Short of breath feelings should start to get better. Walk slowly inside your home for 5 to 10 minutes each day. You can now lift heavier things. Keep wearing your compression stockings and using your CPAP machine every night.

**Week 3-4:**
You should have more energy to move around. Walking outside is now okay. Do not run or do hard exercise yet. Weigh yourself every morning. Call your doctor right away if you gain 3 pounds in one day. Your goal this week is to walk a little farther each day.

**Week 5-6:**
Your body is healing well. You can do light chores around the house. Do not do heavy lifting or yard work yet. Keep using your CPAP machine every night. Go to all your follow-up doctor visits.

**Normal symptoms vs. when to call your doctor:**
Mild tiredness and slow shortness of breath improvement are normal. Call your doctor if shortness of breath gets worse. Call if you gain weight very fast. Call if your legs swell more. Call if you feel dizzy or faint.

**When to expect improvement:**
You will start to feel better in 2 to 3 weeks. Keep taking all your heart medicines as prescribed. Progress is slow but steady.

ESCALATION GUIDE:
CALL 911 IMMEDIATELY
These symptoms are life-threatened. Do not drive yourself.
- Cannot catch your breath while speaking: Your heart is not pumping blood with enough oxygen.
- Chest pressure or tightness that does not go away: This means your heart needs help right now.
- Confusion with cold, clammy skin, or blue lips: Your body is not getting enough oxygen.
- New cough with pink, foamy spit: Fluid is backing up into your lungs. This is dangerous.
- Severe dizziness or passing out: Your heart is not pumping enough blood to your brain.

GO TO THE ER TODAY
Do not wait until tomorrow. Go within a few hours.
- Shortness of breath gets much worse when you lie flat: Fluid may be building up in your lungs.
- Weight gain of 2 pounds or more overnight: This means fluid is building up too fast.

CALL YOUR DOCTOR
Call during office hours. Do not go to the ER for these.

DIAGNOSIS EXPLANATION:
# Understanding Your Lung Condition

## What Happened

Your lungs got worse during your hospital stay. This is called an exacerbation. Your airways become swollen and tight when this happens.

## Why This Happened

You have chronic lung disease. This means your airways stay swollen most of the time. You smoked for many years. That damage does not go away completely.

During your stay, your lungs flared up. They got more swollen and made it hard to breathe.

## What This Means

Your lungs need extra care now. They do not work as well as healthy lungs. Breathing takes more effort for you.

You also have chronic bronchitis. This means your airways make a lot of mucus. The mucus can clog your airways.

## What Helps

You have a high blood pressure. Keep taking your blood pressure medicine. This helps protect your heart and lungs.

You also feel nervous when you cannot breathe. That is normal. Talk to your doctor if the worry gets worse.

Not smoking is the best thing you can do. Quitting smoking stopped more damage to your lungs.

---

**Important:** Take all your medicines as directed. Call your doctor if you cough up blood or feel very short of breath.

MEDICATION RATIONALE:
Prednisone:
This medicine reduces swelling in your airways. That helps you breathe easier during your flare-up. You may feel more hungry or have trouble sleeping. These side effects are normal. Take this medicine for 5 days only. Then taper it as your doctor instructed. Call your doctor if you feel very anxious, have severe mood changes, or see blood in your stool.

Azithromycin:
This antibiotic kills the bacteria causing your infection. Bacteria are germs that can make you sicker. You may have a upset stomach or diarrhea. These side effects usually go away on their own. Complete the full 5-day course even if you feel better. Call your doctor if you have severe diarrhea, fever, or trouble swallowing.

Tiotropium (Spiriva):
This medicine opens your airways every single day. It helps prevent flare-ups of your lung disease. You may taste something metallic in your mouth. This is normal and goes away quickly. Use this medicine every day even when you feel okay. Call your doctor if you have eye pain, blurred vision, or sudden trouble breathing.

Albuterol (ProAir HFA):
This inhaler opens your airways very fast when needed. Use it when you feel short of breath. Your heart may beat a little faster. This is normal and means the medicine is working. You can use this inhaler up to every 4 to 6 hours. Call 911 right away if your breathing does not improve or gets worse.

Fluticasone/Salmeterol (Advair Diskus):
This medicine reduces swelling in your airways all day. It also opens your airways to help you breathe. You may have a sore throat or hoarse voice. These side effects are common and not serious. Use this inhaler twice a day every single day. Rinse your mouth after each use to prevent infection. Call your doctor if you have trouble swallowing or see white patches in your mouth.

Amlodipine:
This medicine helps lower your high blood pressure. It relaxes your blood vessels so blood flows easier. You may have swelling in your ankles or feet. This side effect is common and not dangerous. Take this medicine every day at the same time. Call your doctor if you have chest pain, dizziness, or very swollen ankles.

RECOVERY TRAJECTORY:
**Week 1:**
You will feel tired and short of breath when you move around. This is normal after your hospital stay. Rest at home for the first few days. Start walking slowly — try 5 minutes twice a day inside your home. When you walk or climb stairs, breathe in through your nose and out slowly through your lips — this helps your lungs work better.

Call your doctor if you cough up blood or feel chest pain. Do not lift anything heavy or do hard exercise. Your goal this week is to rest and take your medicines on time.

**Week 2:**
You should feel a little stronger this week. Shortness of breath during light walking should slowly get better. Keep walking a little longer each day — add 2 to 3 more minutes if you feel okay. Stay away from smoke, dust, strong smells, and cold air — these can make your breathing worse.

Do not drive if your medicine makes you feel dizzy or jittery. Do not do any heavy lifting or strenuous activity yet. Call your doctor if you feel more short of breath, not less.

**Week 3-4:**
You can walk longer now and do light activities around your home. Keep using the lip-breathing technique during activity. You can do light chores, but take breaks often. Go to all your follow-up appointments with your doctor.

Avoid cold air and smoke. Do not lift heavy things or exercise hard. Call your doctor right away if you have fever, cough up mucus with blood, or feel chest pain.

**When to expect improvement:**
You should start to feel less short of breath by week 3 or 4. Keep taking all your medicines exactly as prescribed. Progress is slow but steady — be patient with yourself.

ESCALATION GUIDE:
CALL 911 IMMEDIATELY
These symptoms are life-threatening. Do not drive yourself.
- Lips, fingernails, or face turning blue or grey: Your body is not getting enough oxygen right now.
- Breathing does not improve or gets worse after using your rescue inhaler: Your lungs need emergency help.
- Confusion, difficulty speaking, or extreme drowsiness: Your brain is not getting enough oxygen.
- Coughing up blood or blood-tinged mucus: This means your airways are bleeding.
- Unable to complete a full sentence due to shortness of breath: Your lungs are failing.
- Chest pain or rapid, racing heartbeat: This can mean your heart is in danger.

GO TO THE ER TODAY
Do not wait until tomorrow. Go within a few hours.
- Fever above 101 degrees F: This can mean you have a new lung infection.

CALL YOUR DOCTOR
Call during office hours. Do not go to the ER for these.

DIAGNOSIS EXPLANATION:
# Your Diagnosis Explained

## What Happened

Your lungs have a long-term disease called COPD. That means your airways get tight and damaged over time. During your hospital stay, your COPD got much worse. We call that an exacerbation.

## About Your Lungs

You have emphysema. This means the air sacs in your lungs are damaged. Damaged air sacs make it hard to breathe. Your lungs cannot move air in and out the way they should.

## Other Conditions

You also have acid reflux. Stomach acid comes up into your food pipe. This can make your cough worse.

You have sleep apnea. Your breathing stops for short times while you sleep. This can make your lungs work harder.

## Moving Forward

Your doctors treated your COPD flare-up in the hospital. Now you need to rest and get better at home. Follow your discharge plan closely. Take all your medicines as ordered.

If you have trouble breathing, call your doctor right away. Do not wait.

MEDICATION RATIONALE:
Prednisone:
This medicine reduces swelling in your lungs and airways. Your lungs are inflamed from a flare-up of your COPD. You may feel hungrier or have trouble sleeping. These are normal side effects. Call your doctor if you feel very confused, very sad, or have chest pain.

Doxycycline:
This antibiotic fights the infection causing your flare-up. It kills bacteria in your lungs and airways. You may feel a little nausea or have loose stools. Take this medicine with food to help your stomach. Call your doctor if you have trouble swallowing, a rash, or severe belly pain.

Ipratropium/Albuterol:
This inhaler opens your airways so you can breathe easier. It has two medicines that work together to relax your lung muscles. You may feel your heart beating faster or have a dry mouth. This is normal. Call your doctor if you have chest pain, trouble seeing, or urine that is hard to pass.

Budesonide/Formoterol:
This inhaler prevents swelling in your lungs over time. It has a steroid that reduces inflammation and a bronchodilator that opens airways. You may get a sore throat or hoarse voice. Rinse your mouth after each use to prevent these. Call your doctor if you have a high fever, mouth sores, or trouble breathing that gets worse.

Omeprazole:
This medicine protects your stomach from acid. It helps prevent heartburn and ulcers. You may have fewer stomach complaints. This means the medicine is working. Call your doctor if you have severe belly pain, black stools, or vomit that looks like coffee grounds.

Guaifenesin:
This medicine thins the mucus in your lungs. Thinner mucus is easier to cough up. You may feel a little nausea or have loose stools. Drink plenty of water to help this medicine work better. Call your doctor if coughing up blood or severe chest pain happens.

RECOVERY TRAJECTORY:
**Week 1:**
You will feel tired and short of breath. This is normal after your hospital stay. Rest as much as you need. Walk slowly inside your home for 5 to 10 minutes three times a day. Use your oxygen at night and when you move around. Call your doctor if you cough up blood or cannot catch your breath at rest.

**Week 2:**
You may have a little more energy this week. Keep walking indoors at your own pace. Use your oxygen during activity if you feel short of breath. Practice using your inhaler the way the hospital taught you. Your goal this week is to walk a bit longer each day. Call your doctor if you feel worse or very tired.

**Week 3-4:**
You can do light activities around your home. Keep using your oxygen at night. Stay away from crowds and people who are sick. Coughing up mucus is normal. Keep your head of your bed raised up high when you sleep. This helps you breathe better. Go to all your follow-up visits.

**When to expect improvement:**
Feeling less short of breath takes time. Keep taking all your medicines as prescribed. Call your doctor right away if you have a fever or feel much worse.

ESCALATION GUIDE:
CALL 911 IMMEDIATELY
These symptoms are life-threatening. Do not drive yourself.
- Sudden sharp chest pain that does not go away: This can mean your heart is in trouble.
- Extreme difficulty breathing or you cannot catch your breath: Your body is not getting enough oxygen right now.
- Coughing up bloody mucus: This means there is bleeding in your lungs.
- Dizziness or lightheadedness or feeling like you might pass out: Your brain is not getting enough blood flow.
- Using neck or chest muscles hard to breathe: This means your lungs are working way too hard.

GO TO THE ER TODAY
Do not wait until tomorrow. Go within a few hours.
- Coughing up yellow or green mucus that is getting worse: This can mean a lung infection is starting.
- Increased swelling in ankles, feet, or legs: This can mean fluid is building up in your body.
- Shortness of breath even when you use your oxygen: Your lungs need help from doctors right away.
- Rescue inhaler is not helping even when you use it a lot: Your airway needs emergency treatment now.

CALL YOUR DOCTOR
Call during office hours. Do not go to the ER for these.
- Cannot sleep because of shortness of breath: Your doctor can help adjust your breathing treatment plan.

DIAGNOSIS EXPLANATION:
# Your Diagnosis Explained

## What is Type 1 Diabetes?

Your body makes a fluid called insulin. Insulin helps your cells use sugar from food. In Type 1 Diabetes, your body cannot make enough insulin. This means sugar builds up in your blood.

## What You May Have Noticed

You may have felt very thirsty. You may have needed to pee a lot. You may have lost weight without trying. These happen when your blood sugar is too high.

## Why This Happened

When blood sugar gets too high, your body tries to flush it out. That is why you urinate more. Your body also needs more water. That is why you feel thirsty.

## What This Means

Your kidneys and body were working hard. This caused some mild fluid loss. Your doctor has a plan to help you feel better.

## Next Steps

Talk to your doctor about your care plan. Ask questions about anything you do not understand. You will learn how to manage this over time.

MEDICATION RATIONALE:
Insulin glargine:
This medicine gives your body long-acting insulin. Your body needs insulin to control blood sugar. You inject this under your skin at bedtime. You should feel better as your blood sugar improves. Call your doctor if you feel shaky, sweaty, or very tired.

Insulin lispro:
This medicine gives your body fast-acting insulin. You take it right before you eat meals. It helps your body use the food you eat. You should feel better as your blood sugar improves. Call your doctor if you feel shaky, sweaty, or very tired.

Glucagon Emergency Kit:
This is a rescue medicine for very low blood sugar. Only family members should use it for you. They inject it if you cannot eat or drink. Call 911 or go to the ER immediately if you experience severe low blood sugar symptoms.

RECOVERY TRAJECTORY:
**Week 1:**
You just learned you have Type 1 Diabetes. Your blood sugar has been too high. You are starting insulin shots to bring it down. You may feel tired and thirsty right now. This is normal as your body adjusts.

Check your blood sugar before you eat and before bed. Write down all your numbers in your logbook. Take your insulin shots exactly as taught in the hospital. Do not skip any doses. Your goal this week is to get comfortable giving yourself shots.

Keep moving a little bit each day. Walk slowly around your home. Do not do hard exercise yet. Always carry glucose tablets or juice with you. If you feel shaky or sweaty, eat one glucose tablet right away.

Call your doctor if you feel very sick or confused. Call if you throw up or cannot eat. Call if your blood sugar stays above 250 even after taking insulin.

**Week 2:**
You should feel a bit better this week. Your blood sugar numbers should be getting lower. You may still feel a little tired. This is okay. Keep taking your insulin at the same times each day.

Check your blood sugar before and after walking or any movement. Write down these numbers in your logbook. This helps your doctor see if insulin is working right. Your goal this week is to do a short walk every day.

You can walk around your neighborhood if you feel strong enough. Do not run or do hard exercise yet. Always have glucose tablets with you when you leave home. If your blood sugar is above 250 and you have ketones (your doctor will teach you how to check), do not exercise.

Call your doctor if your blood sugar numbers are not getting better. Call if you feel confused or very sleepy. Call if you have belly pain or feel like you might throw up.

**Week 3-4:**
You should feel more like yourself now. Your energy should be getting better. Your blood sugar should be much more stable. Keep taking your insulin shots the same way. Do not change your doses without talking to your doctor.

You can do more normal activities now. Walk farther or do light activities at home. Always check your blood sugar before you exercise. Write down the number. Exercise after you check, then check again when you are done. This tells you how your body responds to movement.

Always carry fast-acting glucose with you. Keep it in your pocket or bag. Make sure your family and friends know where you keep glucose. Show them how to help you if you feel shaky. Make sure they know how to use your Glucagon kit if you ever pass out from low blood sugar.

Call your doctor if your blood sugar goes way up or way down. Call if you feel very sick. Go to all your follow-up doctor visits. Bring your blood sugar logbook to show your doctor.

**When to expect improvement:**
Your blood sugar should keep getting closer to normal over the next few weeks. It takes time for your body to adjust to insulin. Keep checking your blood sugar, taking your medicine, and eating the foods your nutritionist said are best for you.

ESCALATION GUIDE:
CALL 911 IMMEDIATELY
These symptoms are life-threatening. Do not drive yourself.
- Confusion, extreme sleepiness, or loss of consciousness: Your brain needs sugar. This is a medical emergency.
- Rapid, deep breathing or "fruity" smelling breath: Your blood is becoming too acidic. This is very dangerous.
- Presence of moderate to large ketones in urine: Ketones mean your body is breaking down fat too fast. This is life-threatening.
- Severe low blood sugar below 55 mg/dL: Your brain cannot work without sugar. Treat with 15 grams of carbs right now. Call 911 if it does not improve in 15 minutes.

GO TO THE ER TODAY
Do not wait until tomorrow. Go within a few hours.
- Blood sugar level above 400 mg/dL that does not respond to correction insulin: High blood sugar for too long can damage your body. Go to the ER now.
- Persistent vomiting or inability to keep fluids down: You need fluids to stay safe. Vomiting for hours is dangerous.

CALL YOUR DOCTOR
Call during office hours. Do not go to the ER for these.
- No symptoms in this tier for your diagnosis.
