DIAGNOSIS EXPLANATION:
# Understanding Your Diagnosis

## Your Main Condition: Type 2 Diabetes

Your body has trouble controlling blood sugar. Blood sugar is the fuel your body uses for energy.

Normally, your pancreas makes something called insulin. Insulin helps blood sugar get into your cells. With Type 2 diabetes, your body either doesn't make enough insulin. Or your body can't use the insulin well.

Right now, your blood sugar is higher than it should be. This means the insulin isn't working as well as needed.

## Other Conditions You Have

**High Blood Pressure**
Your heart pumps blood through your body. High blood pressure means that blood pushes too hard on your vessels. This can happen with diabetes.

**High Cholesterol**
Cholesterol is a waxy substance in your blood. Too much of it can build up in your blood vessels. This also often happens with diabetes.

**Dehydration**
Your body didn't have enough water during your hospital stay. You may need to drink more water and fluids.

## What This Means

These conditions work together. Controlling your blood sugar helps your heart and blood vessels too. It also helps prevent problems with your kidneys and eyes.

You have the power to feel better. Follow your doctor's plan. Take your medicines as told. Eat healthy foods. Move your body a little each day.

Your doctor will help you manage all of this.

MEDICATION RATIONALE:
Metformin:
This medicine helps your body use sugar better. It lowers the amount of sugar your blood makes. You may have stomach upset or loose stools at first. These side effects usually go away after a few days. Call your doctor if you have severe stomach pain, vomiting, or trouble breathing.

Glimepiride:
This medicine helps your pancreas release more insulin. Insulin is a hormone that lowers your blood sugar. You may feel shaky, sweaty, or hungry if your sugar drops too low. Eat a small snack with sugar right away if this happens. Call your doctor if you feel very dizzy, confused, or lose consciousness.

Insulin glargine:
This medicine gives your body the insulin it needs to lower blood sugar. You inject it under your skin once each night before bed. You may have redness, swelling, or bruising where you inject. Rotating injection sites helps prevent this. Call your doctor if you have signs of low blood sugar like shaking, sweating, or confusion. Also call if you have redness that does not go away or signs of infection at the injection site.

Lisinopril:
This medicine helps lower your blood pressure. It also protects your heart and kidneys from diabetes damage. You may feel lightheaded or have a dry cough. These side effects are common and usually improve over time. Call your doctor if your face or lips swell, you have trouble breathing, or your cough gets worse.

Atorvastatin:
This medicine lowers cholesterol in your blood. High cholesterol can damage your heart and blood vessels. You may have muscle aches or stomach upset. These side effects are usually mild and temporary. Call your doctor if you have severe muscle pain, dark urine, or yellowing of your skin.

RECOVERY TRAJECTORY:
**Week 1:**
Your blood sugar was very high when you came to the hospital. The doctors started insulin to help bring it down. You will feel tired as your body adjusts to the new medicine. Walk for 10 to 15 minutes each day inside your home. Check your feet every day for red spots, blisters, or cuts. Always wear shoes, even inside your house.

**Week 2:**
You should have more energy this week. Keep checking your blood sugar as the nurse showed you. Walk a little longer each day. Your goal is to walk for 20 to 30 minutes by the end of this week. Check your blood sugar before you drive or use any machinery. Wear proper shoes at all times.

**Week 3-4:**
Continue your daily walks for 20 to 30 minutes. Check your feet every single day using a mirror if you cannot bend down easily. Wear shoes that fit well and feel comfortable. Do not walk barefoot anywhere, not even in your home. Check your blood sugar before driving. Call your doctor if you feel dizzy or very tired.

**Week 5-6:**
Keep up your walking routine. You can add light activities like gentle stretching or slow yard work. Keep checking your feet daily and wearing proper shoes. Your blood sugar numbers should be getting better each week. Call your doctor if your blood sugar stays very high. Attend all your follow-up appointments.

**When to expect improvement:**
Your blood sugar will come down slowly over several weeks. Keep taking all your medicines exactly as prescribed. Progress takes time, but you will feel better as your numbers improve.

ESCALATION GUIDE:
CALL 911 IMMEDIATELY
These symptoms are life-threatening. Do not drive yourself.
- Blood sugar above 400 mg/dL or "HI" reading: This can mean diabetic emergency. Get help right now.
- Severe low blood sugar with confusion or sweating: Your brain needs sugar. This is an emergency.
- Shortness of breath or chest pain: Your heart may not be getting enough blood.
- Sudden blurred vision: This can mean a stroke or serious eye problem.

GO TO THE ER TODAY
Do not wait until tomorrow. Go within a few hours.
- Non-healing wounds or sores on feet or legs: These can get infected very fast with diabetes.
- Significant redness on feet or legs: This can mean infection or poor blood flow.

CALL YOUR DOCTOR
Call during office hours. Do not go to the ER for these.
- Numbness or tingling in hands or feet: This can happen with diabetes but your doctor should know.
- Extreme thirst and frequent urination: Tell your doctor even though you take your medicines.

DIAGNOSIS EXPLANATION:
# Your Hip and Health

## What Happened to Your Hip

Your right hip joint had severe arthritis. Arthritis means the smooth lining inside the joint wore away. This caused pain and stiffness.

During your hospital stay, doctors replaced your hip joint with a new one. This surgery is called a hip replacement. The new hip should work much better.

## Other Health Conditions

You have diabetes. Your diabetes is doing well right now. Keep taking your oral medications as prescribed.

You also have high blood pressure. Your blood pressure is being managed with medicine.

You have mild low blood count. This means you have fewer red blood cells than normal. This was noticed before your surgery. Your doctor will check this again.

You carry extra weight. Extra weight can put stress on your new hip. Staying active and eating well helps your recovery.

## What This Means

Your new hip should give you less pain. You should be able to move better over time. Follow your doctor's orders for physical therapy. This will help your hip heal and get stronger.

Keep taking all your medicines. Call your doctor if you have new pain, swelling, or trouble moving your hip.

MEDICATION RATIONALE:
Enoxaparin:
This medicine thins your blood to prevent clots. Blood clots can form after hip surgery or with immobility. You inject this medicine under your skin once a day. You may see small bruises where you inject. This is normal. Call your doctor if you have unusual bleeding, severe bruising, or black stools.

Celecoxib:
This medicine reduces swelling and pain in your hip. It helps you move more comfortably during healing. You may have an upset stomach or heartburn. Take it with food to help prevent this. Call your doctor if you have stomach pain, bloody stools, or signs of an allergic reaction.

Oxycodone:
This medicine is a strong pain reliever for severe pain. Use it when your pain is very bad. You may feel drowsy or dizzy after taking it. Do not drive or operate machinery after taking this medicine. Call your doctor if you feel confused, have trouble breathing, or notice your pain is not better.

Metformin:
This medicine helps control your blood sugar. You have been taking this medicine before. Keep taking it the same way. You may have a slight upset stomach. This usually goes away over time. Call your doctor if you have severe stomach pain or vomiting.

Lisinopril:
This medicine helps your heart and blood pressure. You have been taking this medicine before. Keep taking it the same way. You may have a dry cough. This is common and not dangerous. Call your doctor if your face or lips swell, or if you feel very dizzy.

Pantoprazole:
This medicine protects your stomach from acid. You have been taking this medicine before. Keep taking it the same way. You may have mild headaches or bloating. These side effects are not serious. Call your doctor if you have severe stomach pain or black stools.

RECOVERY TRAJECTORY:
**Week 1:**
You will feel sore and very tired right now. This is normal after hip surgery. Your hip needs to rest and heal. Do not bend your hip more than 90 degrees — keep your knee lower than your hip at all times. Do not cross your legs or twist your body at the hips.

Use your front-wheeled walker for all walking. Walk short distances indoors only right now. Every hour while you are awake, pump your ankles up and down 10 times. This keeps blood clots from forming. Do the leg squeezes your therapist showed you three times each day — 10 squeezes each time.

Pain and swelling are normal this week. Swelling may get worse before it gets better. Call your doctor if you have fever, sudden sharp pain, or redness around your incision. Your goal this week is to rest and follow all the walker rules without cheating.

**Week 2:**
You may still feel sore and tired. Walking with your walker should feel easier now. Continue using your walker for all walking. Keep doing your ankle pumps every hour.

Do your leg exercises three times each day. Slide your heel along the bed slowly. Do straight leg raises as your therapist showed you. Do not do any new exercises without asking your therapist first. Swelling may still be present — this is okay.

Walking short distances around your home is fine. Stairs are not safe yet — avoid them. Do not sit with your hip bent more than 90 degrees. Sit in firm chairs with armrests only. Call your doctor if pain gets worse or does not improve with rest.

**Week 3-4:**
Your strength should be getting better now. Walking with your walker should feel more steady. Your therapist may let you try a cane for short walks indoors. Use the cane on the side opposite your surgery.

Keep doing all your exercises every day. Swelling should start to go down. You can do light activities around the house. Avoid climbing stairs, heavy lifting, and driving. Do not bend your hip more than 90 degrees. This rule is still very important.

Sit in firm chairs only — avoid soft couches. Keep your knees apart with a pillow between your legs when you sleep. Your goal this week is to walk a little farther each day with your walker or cane. Call your doctor if you fall or your pain gets much worse.

**When to expect improvement:**
You should feel stronger and have less pain by week 4. Your goal is to use a cane by week 6 without the walker. Keep doing your exercises and follow hip precautions at home. Progress takes time. Do not rush.

ESCALATION GUIDE:
CALL 911 IMMEDIATELY
These symptoms are life-threatening. Do not drive yourself.
- Sudden shortness of breath or chest pain: These signs can mean a blood clot in your lung.
- Rapid heart rate with chest pain: Your heart may not be getting enough blood right now.
- Sudden severe hip pain with visible deformity: Your new hip may have slipped out of place.
- Inability to bear weight after sudden hip pain: This means your hip joint is not stable.
- Extreme drowsiness or slow breathing: Your pain medicine may be too strong for your body.
- Stopped breathing: Call 911 right now. Do not wait.

GO TO THE ER TODAY
Do not wait until tomorrow. Go within a few hours.
- Calf pain, swelling, or redness in either leg: This can be a sign of a blood clot.
- High fever above 101.5 degrees F that will not go away: Your body may have an infection.
- Increased pain, redness, or warmth around your incision: These are signs your incision may be getting infected.
- Foul-smelling drainage from your incision: This means bacteria may be growing in your wound.

CALL YOUR DOCTOR
Call during office hours. Do not go to the ER for these.
- Numbness or tingling in your operated leg: Your nerves may be adjusting to the new hip.
- Weakness in your operated leg: This can happen during healing but your doctor should know.

DIAGNOSIS EXPLANATION:
# Your Primary Diagnosis Explained

## What Happened to Your Hip

Your left hip bone was damaged. The bone did not get enough blood flow. When bone does not get blood, it can die. This is called avascular necrosis. It means "death of bone from no blood."

## Why This Matters

A dead hip bone causes pain. It can break down over time. Your doctors decided the best fix was surgery.

## What Your Surgery Did

Your doctors replaced your damaged hip bone. They put in a new hip joint. This new joint works like a normal hip. It should help you move better and have less pain.

## Your Other Health Issues

You also have kidney disease. Your kidneys do not filter blood as well as they should. But your kidney function is stable right now.

You have high cholesterol. Extra cholesterol in your blood is not good for your heart. Medicine can help lower it.

Your stomach refluxes sometimes. That means acid comes up from your stomach. This can cause discomfort.

You also feel sad sometimes. Medicine helps keep your mood stable.

---

**Next Steps:** Follow up with your doctor about your hip recovery. Keep taking your medicines as prescribed.

MEDICATION RATIONALE:
Aspirin:
This medicine helps prevent blood clots from forming. Blood clots can happen after surgery or from staying still. You may notice mild stomach upset or heartburn. Take it with food if your stomach feels uncomfortable. Call your doctor if you have severe stomach pain, black stools, or unusual bleeding.

Gabapentin:
This medicine helps calm nerve pain in your hip. Nerve pain feels like burning, tingling, or shooting sensations. You may feel drowsy or dizzy, especially at first. This usually gets better as your body adjusts to the medicine. Call your doctor if you feel very confused, have trouble with balance, or feel extremely drowsy.

Tramadol:
This medicine reduces pain from your hip condition. Take it only when you have pain, not on a schedule. You may feel dizzy, drowsy, or a bit foggy. This is normal and means the medicine is working. Call your doctor if you feel very confused, have trouble breathing, or notice a rash on your skin.

Atorvastatin:
This medicine helps lower cholesterol in your blood. High cholesterol can harm your heart over time. You may not feel any change, but the medicine is still working. Most people have no side effects from this medicine. Call your doctor if you have muscle pain or weakness that is new or bothersome.

Omeprazole:
This medicine reduces acid in your stomach. It protects your stomach from pain and upset. You may have fewer stomach symptoms after taking it. This medicine is very safe for most people. Call your doctor if you have severe stomach pain or trouble swallowing.

Sertraline:
This medicine helps improve your mood and reduce anxiety. Your hip pain and surgery recovery can be stressful on your mind. You may notice your mood improves over a few weeks. Some people feel a bit nauseous at first, but this usually passes. Call your doctor if you feel very anxious, have trouble sleeping, or notice unusual changes in your mood.

RECOVERY TRAJECTORY:
**Week 1:**
You will feel very tired and sore. This is normal after surgery. You can put your full weight on your new hip when you walk. Do not do any jumping or running. Every hour while awake, pump your ankles up and down. This keeps blood clots from forming.

**Week 2:**
You may have less pain now. Walking should feel a little easier. Keep pumping your ankles every hour. Do your leg squeezes and thigh tightening exercises twice a day. Do 3 sets of 15 each time. Do not bend your hip more than 90 degrees. Call your doctor if your leg swells or feels warm.

**Week 3-4:**
You can walk more around your house and yard. Your physical therapist will teach you hip exercises. Do these exercises every day as shown. You can stand and move your hip out to the side. You can also move your hip backward. Avoid any jumping, running, or hard twisting. Do not lift anything heavy.

**Week 5-6:**
You should feel stronger now. Walking should be easier and less painful. Keep doing your home exercises every day. Talk to your surgeon before using the stationary bike. If your surgeon says yes, start the bike slowly. Increase time by a little bit each day.

**Week 7 and beyond:**
You can do more normal activities. Avoid high-impact sports like running or jumping. Swimming and walking are good for you. Keep doing your exercises to stay strong. Go to all your follow-up appointments with your surgeon.

**When to expect improvement:**
You will feel better each week. Most healing takes 3 to 6 months. Keep taking your medicines as prescribed. Call your doctor right away if you have sudden swelling, warmth, or a fever.

ESCALATION GUIDE:
CALL 911 IMMEDIATELY
These symptoms are life-threatening. Do not drive yourself.
- Sudden chest pain or shortness of breath: This can mean a blood clot reached your lungs.
- Coughing up blood: This is a sign of serious bleeding in your lungs.
- Sudden loss of ability to walk: Your hip may have popped out of place.
- Severe hip pain with visible deformity: Your new hip joint may be dislocated.
- Throat swelling or difficulty breathing: This is a severe allergic reaction to a medicine.

GO TO THE ER TODAY
Do not wait until tomorrow. Go within a few hours.
- One-sided leg swelling, pain, or redness in calf: This can mean a blood clot in your leg.
- Fever above 101 degrees F lasting more than 24 hours: Your body may be fighting a serious infection.
- Increased pain at incision with pus or foul odor: Your incision may be infected.
- Increased pain at incision with warmth: Heat with pain means possible infection.
- Hives or rash all over your body: This is an allergic reaction to a medicine.

CALL YOUR DOCTOR
Call during office hours. Do not go to the ER for these.
- Decreased urine output: Your kidneys may not be working as well today.
- Significant swelling in hands or feet: Your body may be holding too much fluid.

# Your Diagnosis Explained

## Your Main Problem: Gallbladder Infection

You have an infection in your gallbladder. This happened because small stones got stuck inside it.

Your gallbladder is a small bag. It sits under your liver. It stores fluid that helps you digest food.

When stones block the opening, fluid gets trapped. This causes pain and swelling. The trapped area can get infected.

## What Else Is Going On

You also have **diabetes**. This means your body has trouble controlling blood sugar levels.

You have **high blood pressure** too. Your blood pushes too hard against your vessel walls.

After surgery, your stomach and gut moved slower than normal. This is common after operations. It usually gets better on its own.

## What Happens Next

Your doctors found the stones. They also found a stone stuck in a tube that drains bile. This tube needed attention too.

Rest at home. Follow any eating plans your doctor gave you. Take your medicines as prescribed.

Call your doctor if you have belly pain, fever, or throwing up.

MEDICATION RATIONALE:
Metformin:
This medicine helps control your blood sugar levels. You take it twice daily with meals. You may notice no change in how you feel. This is normal. Call your doctor if you have severe nausea, vomiting, or stomach pain.

Lisinopril:
This medicine helps lower your blood pressure. It makes it easier for your heart to pump. You may feel a little dizzy at first. This usually goes away. Call your doctor if you have severe dizziness, fainting, or swelling in your face or lips.

Oxycodone/Acetaminophen:
This medicine treats severe pain from your gallbladder surgery. Take it every 6 hours only if you have bad pain. You may feel sleepy or dizzy. You may also feel constipated. Do not drive or use machines while taking this. Call your doctor if you have trouble breathing, severe drowsiness, or confusion.

Ibuprofen:
This medicine reduces pain and swelling after your oxycodone course ends. Take it every 8 hours with food to protect your stomach. You may have mild stomach upset. This usually goes away. Call your doctor if you have stomach pain, black stools, or vomiting blood.

Metronidazole (Flagyl):
This medicine fights the infection in your gallbladder. You must take all five days of this medicine. Finish the complete course even if you feel better. Your mouth may taste metallic or bitter. This is normal. Call your doctor if you have severe nausea, trouble swallowing, or numbness in your hands or feet.

Ondansetron (Zofran):
This medicine stops nausea and vomiting. Take it every 8 hours only when you feel sick. You may feel a little constipated. This is normal. Call your doctor if nausea does not improve or you cannot keep food down.

Docusate Sodium:
This medicine keeps you from getting constipated. Take it twice daily. It softens your stool so it is easier to pass. You may see results within one or two days. Call your doctor if you have severe stomach cramping or no bowel movement after three days.

Omeprazole:
This medicine protects your stomach lining from acid. Take it once daily before breakfast. You may have fewer stomach problems. This is good. Call your doctor if you have severe stomach pain, black stools, or vomiting that looks like coffee grounds.

RECOVERY TRAJECTORY:
**Week 1:**
You will feel very tired and sore after surgery. This is normal. Your belly will have an incision with staples that needs to stay clean and dry. Do not lift anything heavier than a cup of milk. Walk slowly around your home a few times each day.

You can shower after 48 hours. Pat your incision dry with a clean towel. Do not take baths or get in a pool. Pain and tenderness around your incision are normal. Call your doctor if you see pus, redness, or warmth around the staples.

**Week 2:**
You should feel a little less sore this week. Walking short distances is good for you. Do not lift anything over 5 pounds yet. Do not drive because pain medicine can make you drowsy. Your goal this week is to walk a little further each day.

Your incision may still drain a little. This is okay. Swelling and bruising around your belly are normal. Call your doctor if fever, warmth, or spreading redness appear. Also call if pain gets much worse.

**Week 3-4:**
You can do light activities around your home. Walking should feel easier now. Keep your incision clean and dry until staples come out. Do not lift more than 5 pounds. Avoid hard exercise or yard work.

Your incision should start to look better. Itching as it heals is normal. You can drive now if you are not taking pain medicine. Go to your follow-up visit to have staples removed. Call your doctor if you feel worse instead of better.

Your incision will heal slowly over several weeks. Energy and strength return bit by bit. Take all medicines as your doctor prescribed and keep all appointments.
